section.title: The Scroll Burned by Jehoiakim
section.sectionRationale: God orders Jeremiah's decades of oracles gathered onto a single scroll, one last appeal to a nation and a king who still might turn. Baruch reads it in the temple, then before the officials, then before King Jehoiakim himself, who slices the scroll apart column by column and feeds it to the fire in his winter room. The chapter turns on what happens next: God simply orders it written again, longer than before, with judgment now added on the king who tried to destroy it.
section.thesis: Jehoiakim can burn the parchment but not the word: he cuts and burns every column without fear, yet God commands the scroll rewritten and enlarged and pronounces personal judgment on the very king who tried to silence it, proving that His spoken word outlasts every attempt to destroy it.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: the scroll commanded and written at Jeremiah's dictation; Baruch reading it in the temple and then before the officials; Jehoiakim burning it column by column in his winter quarters; and the scroll rewritten, with judgment now pronounced on the king himself.

units.label: The scroll commanded and written
units.note: one last appeal before the disaster

verses.text: In the fourth year of Jehoiakim son of Josiah king of Judah, this word came to Jeremiah from the LORD:
“Take a {{megillah|scroll}} and write on it all the words I have spoken to you concerning Israel, Judah, and all the nations, from the day I first spoke to you during the reign of Josiah until today.
Perhaps when the people of Judah hear about all the calamity I plan to bring upon them, each of them will {{shuv|turn}} from his wicked way. Then I will forgive their iniquity and their sin.”
So Jeremiah called Baruch son of Neriah, and at the dictation of Jeremiah, Baruch wrote on a scroll all the words that the LORD had spoken to Jeremiah.
Then Jeremiah commanded Baruch, “I am restricted; I cannot enter the house of the LORD;

so you are to go to the house of the LORD on a day of fasting, and in the hearing of the people you are to read the words of the LORD from the scroll you have written at my dictation. Read them in the hearing of all the people of Judah who are coming from their cities.
Perhaps they will bring their petition before the LORD, and each one will turn from his wicked way; for great are the anger and fury that the LORD has pronounced against this people.”
So Baruch son of Neriah did everything that Jeremiah the prophet had commanded him. In the house of the LORD he read the words of the LORD from the scroll.

units.label: Read in the temple, then before the officials
units.note: the word spreads from the assembly to the palace

verses.text: Now in the ninth month of the fifth year of Jehoiakim son of Josiah king of Judah, a fast before the LORD was proclaimed to all the people of Jerusalem and all who had come there from the cities of Judah.

From the chamber of Gemariah son of Shaphan the scribe, which was in the upper courtyard at the opening of the New Gate of the house of the LORD, Baruch read from the scroll the words of Jeremiah in the hearing of all the people.
When Micaiah son of Gemariah, the son of Shaphan, heard all the words of the LORD from the scroll,

he went down to the scribe's chamber in the king's palace, where all the officials were sitting: Elishama the scribe, Delaiah son of Shemaiah, Elnathan son of Achbor, Gemariah son of Shaphan, Zedekiah son of Hananiah, and all the other officials.
And Micaiah reported to them all the words he had heard Baruch read from the scroll in the hearing of the people.

Then all the officials sent word to Baruch through Jehudi son of Nethaniah, the son of Shelemiah, the son of Cushi, saying, “Bring the scroll that you read in the hearing of the people, and come here.” 
So Baruch son of Neriah took the scroll and went to them.

“Please sit down,” they said, “and read it in our hearing.” 
So Baruch read it in their hearing.
When they had heard all these words, they turned to one another in fear and said to Baruch, “Surely we must report all these words to the king.”
“Tell us now,” they asked Baruch, “how did you write all these words? Was it at Jeremiah's dictation?”
“It was at his dictation,” Baruch replied. “He recited all these words to me and I wrote them in ink on the scroll.”
Then the officials said to Baruch, “You and Jeremiah must hide yourselves and tell no one where you are.”

units.label: Jehoiakim burns the scroll
units.note: a king cuts and burns what he will not obey

verses.text: So the officials went to the king in the courtyard. And having stored the scroll in the chamber of Elishama the scribe, they reported everything to the king.
Then the king sent Jehudi to get the scroll, and he took it from the chamber of Elishama the scribe. And Jehudi read it in the hearing of the king and all the officials who were standing beside him.
Since it was the ninth month, the king was sitting in his winter quarters with a fire burning before him.
And as soon as Jehudi had read three or four columns, Jehoiakim would cut them off with a scribe's knife and throw them into the firepot, until the entire scroll had been consumed by the fire.
Yet in hearing all these words, the king and his servants did not become frightened or tear their garments.
Even though Elnathan, Delaiah, and Gemariah urged the king not to burn the scroll, he would not listen to them.

Instead, the king commanded Jerahmeel, a son of the king, as well as Seraiah son of Azriel and Shelemiah son of Abdeel, to seize Baruch the scribe and Jeremiah the prophet. But the LORD had hidden them.

units.label: The word rewritten and Jehoiakim judged
units.note: fire cannot destroy what God has spoken

verses.text: After the king had burned the scroll containing the words that Baruch had written at Jeremiah's dictation, the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah:
“Take another scroll and rewrite on it the very words that were on the original scroll, which Jehoiakim king of Judah has burned.

You are to proclaim concerning Jehoiakim king of Judah that this is what the LORD says: You have burned the scroll and said, ‘Why have you written on it that the king of Babylon would surely come and destroy this land and deprive it of man and beast?’
Therefore this is what the LORD says about Jehoiakim king of Judah: He will have no one to sit on David's throne, and his body will be thrown out and exposed to heat by day and frost by night.

I will punish him and his descendants and servants for their iniquity. I will bring on them, on the residents of Jerusalem, and on the men of Judah, all the calamity about which I warned them but they did not listen.”

Then Jeremiah took another scroll and gave it to the scribe Baruch son of Neriah, and at Jeremiah's dictation he wrote on it all the words of the scroll that Jehoiakim king of Judah had burned in the fire. And many similar words were added to them.

megillah.gloss: A scroll, a rolled sheet of leather or papyrus for writing. God commands decades of oracles gathered onto a single scroll as one last, comprehensive appeal, so that the whole weight of the warning could be heard and read at once rather than piecemeal.

shuv.gloss: To turn or return, the Old Testament's ordinary word for repentance. The scroll is written and read for exactly this purpose, that hearing all the warnings together might turn even one wicked heart back to the LORD before disaster falls.

basic.context: God tells Jeremiah to gather everything he has preached since the days of Josiah onto one scroll, one final, comprehensive appeal before disaster falls. Baruch writes it and reads it publicly, first in the temple, then before the royal officials, who are alarmed enough to warn Baruch and Jeremiah to hide. When King Jehoiakim hears it read, he calmly slices off each column with a scribe's knife and burns it in his fire pot, unmoved and unafraid. God's response is to have it rewritten, longer than before, with a specific judgment now added on Jehoiakim himself.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a narrative about the nature of God's word, not merely about one scroll: read literally, it teaches that God's spoken word is durable in a way physical documents are not. Jehoiakim's calm, methodical burning contrasts sharply with the officials' fear, showing hardened unbelief can sit undisturbed in the very presence of a clear warning. The scroll's rewriting, expanded rather than diminished, is the chapter's real punch line.
basic.originalLanguages: The megillah, scroll, is simply the physical medium, a rolled sheet that can be cut and burned column by column. The purpose of writing it is that people might shuv, turn, from their wicked way. The knife that cuts the scroll and the fire that consumes it stand for every human power raised against God's revealed word, and both fail to accomplish what the king intends.
basic.typology: The king's knife and fire, wielded against a written word that keeps returning in fuller form, anticipates the far greater hostility later raised against the Word made flesh, and the same pattern of apparent defeat followed by vindication.
basic.christ: Isaiah had already said the word of our God stands forever, and Jesus later says heaven and earth will pass away but His words will never pass away. Jehoiakim's burned and rewritten scroll is a small, vivid enactment of that truth: human power can destroy parchment but not the word itself. The pattern reaches its fullest expression when the Word made flesh is put to death and buried, yet rises, His word and His person both proved indestructible.
basic.god: God is patient, sending a final, gathered appeal in hope that even now the nation might repent, and He forgives readily wherever people turn. Yet He is not mocked: when the king answers mercy with contempt, cutting and burning what he refuses to obey, God simply restores His word in fuller form and adds a specific, personal judgment on the one who tried to destroy it.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 40:8 says the word of our God stands forever. Matthew 24:35 has Jesus say heaven and earth will pass away but His words will never pass away. Psalm 2 shows kings taking counsel together against the LORD and being answered from heaven. 1 Peter 1:24-25 quotes Isaiah to the same effect, that the word of the Lord endures forever.
basic.oneStory: The scroll burned by Jehoiakim is a small, concrete picture of a truth that runs through the whole Bible: every human attempt to silence God's word only proves its indestructibility, since God simply speaks it again, fuller than before, until it reaches its final form in Christ, the Word who was put to death and rose.

context.literary: A tightly told narrative of dictation, reading, and destruction, moving from temple to palace to the king's private winter quarters, then closing with a second dictation that answers and reverses the first scene's destruction.
context.historical: Set in the fourth and fifth years of Jehoiakim, roughly 605 to 604 BC, shortly after Babylon's victory at Carchemish had made Nebuchadnezzar the dominant power in the region and Judah's coming subjugation increasingly plain.
context.canonical: One of Scripture's clearest narrated demonstrations that God's spoken word cannot be destroyed by human power, a theme that runs from the burning bush to the empty tomb.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To demonstrate concretely that God's word, once spoken, cannot be undone by any human power, however deliberately or calmly that power is exercised, and to pronounce specific judgment on Jehoiakim for his contemptuous rejection of it.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a specific historical narrative describing one king's response to one scroll; it does not prescribe a ritual for handling Scripture. The enduring truth it establishes is that God's word outlasts every attempt to destroy it, and that rejecting it once heard carries real, personal consequences.

debates.question: Why does Jeremiah dictate the scroll rather than write it himself?

views.label: Practical restriction
views.case: Verse 5 states plainly that Jeremiah was restricted from entering the temple, so Baruch's role as scribe and reader was a practical necessity, not a theological statement about Jeremiah's authority.

views.label: A partnership modeling shared ministry
views.case: The chapter goes out of its way to name Baruch repeatedly and credit his exact obedience, suggesting the narrative also wants readers to see faithful, costly partnership in ministry, since Baruch risks the same danger as Jeremiah for delivering the same word.

debates.bearing: Either way, the word's authority rests in its divine source, not in who physically pens or reads it, and faithful transmission of that word can cost the messenger as much as the original prophet.

study.originalLanguages: The megillah, scroll, is simply parchment, vulnerable to a scribe's knife and a fire pot, yet it carries a word meant to move people to shuv, turn, from evil. Jehoiakim's fire consumes the medium entirely, three or four columns at a time, but the message it carried is dictated again, word for word, with more added, showing the gap between a document's fragility and a word's permanence.

typology.type: The scroll cut and burned yet rewritten in fuller form, Jeremiah 36:23, 28, 32
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the pattern completed at the cross and empty tomb, where the Word made flesh is put to death yet rises, proving that no human power can finally silence what God has spoken, Matthew 24:35, 1 Peter 1:24-25.

study.christ: Isaiah 40:8 declares that the word of our God stands forever, and Jesus applies exactly this permanence to His own words in Matthew 24:35. Jehoiakim's methodical destruction of the scroll, followed immediately by its restoration in expanded form, is a vivid small scale enactment of a truth fulfilled at the cross and resurrection: every attempt to destroy God's word or His Word incarnate ends not in silence but in a fuller, more permanent vindication.
study.god: God is patient and merciful, commanding one last, gathered appeal specifically so that people might repent and be forgiven, verse 3. Yet He is also just and unshakeable: when Jehoiakim answers that mercy with calculated contempt, God does not merely restore the message, He adds a specific judgment naming the king by name and describing his ignoble end, showing that rejecting God's patience does not leave a person merely where they started but under a heavier verdict.

crossReferences.note: The grass withers and the flower fades, but the word of our God stands forever, the same truth the burned and rewritten scroll dramatizes.
Jesus applies the same indestructible permanence to His own words, heaven and earth will pass away but My words will never pass away.
Kings and rulers take counsel together against the LORD and His anointed, only to be answered and overruled from heaven, the same pattern as Jehoiakim's futile burning.
Quotes Isaiah 40 to describe the enduring word of the Lord that has been preached as the gospel, the same permanence now attached to the good news of Christ.

study.oneStory: The scroll burned by Jehoiakim is a vivid, small scale rehearsal of the Bible's larger pattern: human power raised against God's word only proves that word's permanence, since God simply speaks it again, fuller than before. That pattern reaches its climax in Christ, the Word made flesh, whom human power puts to death and buries, only for Him to rise, His word and His person both proved beyond the reach of any knife or fire.

quiz.q: Why does God command Jeremiah to have all his oracles gathered onto one scroll?

options.text: So the people would hear the whole warning at once, and perhaps turn from their wicked ways and be forgiven.
options.note: Verse 3 states this purpose directly, that hearing it might lead each person to repent.

options.text: So the priests would have an official copy for temple records.
options.note: The scroll is read publicly to move hearts to repentance, not filed as an archival record.

options.text: Because Jeremiah could no longer speak and needed a written substitute.
options.note: The text says Jeremiah was restricted from entering the temple, not that he could no longer speak.

options.text: So Baruch could sell copies of the prophecies.
options.note: Baruch does write and read the scroll, but the stated purpose is repentance, not distribution or sale.

quiz.q: How does King Jehoiakim respond when he hears the scroll read?

options.text: He calmly cuts off each column with a scribe's knife and burns it, unafraid, ignoring officials who urge him not to.
options.note: Verses 23 to 25 record his methodical, unmoved destruction of the scroll despite warnings.

options.text: He tears his garments in fear and repents immediately.
options.note: Verse 24 explicitly states neither he nor his servants were frightened or tore their garments.

options.text: He orders the scroll preserved in the temple treasury.
options.note: He does the opposite, destroying the scroll entirely in his fire pot.

options.text: He asks Jeremiah to explain the scroll's meaning before deciding what to do.
options.note: He never consults Jeremiah directly; he sends Jehudi to fetch and read the scroll, then destroys it.

quiz.q: What happens to Baruch and Jeremiah after the scroll is burned?

options.text: The LORD hides them from the king's men sent to seize them.
options.note: Verse 26 states plainly that the LORD had hidden them from Jerahmeel and the others sent to arrest them.

options.text: They are captured and imprisoned immediately.
options.note: The text says the opposite, the LORD hid them from capture.

options.text: They flee to Egypt for permanent safety.
options.note: The chapter does not describe a flight to Egypt; that comes later in the book, after Jerusalem falls.

options.text: They are honored by the king for their honesty.
options.note: The king seeks to seize them, not honor them, after burning their scroll.

quiz.q: What specific judgment does God pronounce on Jehoiakim after the burning?

options.text: He will have no one to sit on David's throne, and his body will be cast out, exposed to heat and frost.
options.note: Verse 30 pronounces this specific, personal judgment naming Jehoiakim directly.

options.text: He will be forgiven since the officials tried to stop him.
options.note: The text pronounces judgment on Jehoiakim personally, despite the officials' efforts.

options.text: His descendants will reign in unbroken peace.
options.note: The judgment says the opposite, no one from his line will sit on David's throne.

options.text: He will be exiled to Babylon in chains like Zedekiah later was.
options.note: The specific judgment given here is about an undignified death and burial, not exile in chains.

quiz.q: What happens to the scroll's content after Jehoiakim burns it?

options.text: Jeremiah dictates it again to Baruch on a new scroll, with many similar words added to it.
options.note: Verse 32 records that the words were rewritten in full, and more were added.

options.text: It is lost forever, since Jehoiakim destroyed the only copy.
options.note: The chapter's whole point is that the word is restored, not lost.

options.text: Only a partial summary is later reconstructed from memory.
options.note: The text says all the former words were rewritten, not merely a partial summary.

options.text: God tells Jeremiah the words no longer need to be written down.
options.note: God commands the opposite, that Jeremiah rewrite the very words that were burned.

questions: Jehoiakim heard the word clearly and destroyed it without fear rather than repenting. Where might you be tempted to dismiss a hard truth from God rather than let it change you?
The officials trembled at the scroll's words while the king did not. What does it look like for you to let conviction actually move you toward God rather than fading into mere alarm?

God's response to the burned scroll was simply to have it rewritten, fuller than before. Where do you need to trust that God's word and God's purposes cannot be undone by opposition or setbacks in your own life?
Baruch risked real danger to write and read faithfully what God had spoken through Jeremiah. Who in your life needs you to pass on a hard but true word, even at some cost to yourself?

This chapter is a small picture of the larger truth fulfilled in Christ, that no human power can finally silence God's word. How does the indestructibility of God's word give you confidence this week?

translationNotes.note: The fifth year of Jehoiakim places this fast in the winter of 604 to 603 BC, likely occasioned by fear after Babylon's recent campaigns in the region, explaining why the people gathered to fast before the LORD.
The scribe's knife used to cut the columns was an ordinary tool for trimming and sharpening reed pens, here turned to the grim purpose of dismembering the scroll piece by piece before burning it.

And many similar words were added to them likely refers to further oracles Jeremiah had delivered in the meantime, or additional detail expanding the original content, showing the second scroll was not merely a copy but an enlargement.
